content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 18, 2018

Dennis Sirkin
1711 W. Caldwell Avenue
Visalia, CA 93277

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-238

Dear Mr. Sirkin:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act")¹ and Section 1090.² Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including common law conflict of interest.

We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Are you required to step down as a board member of the Visalia Veteran's Memorial District if you were to marry the District General Manager?

CONCLUSION

Neither Section 1090 nor the Act require that you step down. However, Section 1090 prohibits you and the Board from changing your spouse's terms of employment or amending her employment contract. To the extent that the Board must renew or terminate your spouse's contract, the Board should seek further advice. Moreover, the Act prohibits you from taking part in any decision with a foreseeable and material effect on your personal finances. To the extent that a

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.

File No. A-17-238
Page No. 2

decision implicates your spouse or her salary, you should seek further advice before taking part in
the decision.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You are the Chair of the Board of Directors of the Visalia Veteran's Memorial District. The
District employs a General Manager who oversees the daily operations of the District. The General
Manager answers to the Board of Directors who sets forth the duties, performance, pay, and
benefits of the General Manager. You and the General Manager are contemplating marriage.

You informed that the General Manager has not held her position for a year prior to your
election. You will be up for reelection in approximately two-and-a-half years.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090:

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Under Section 1090, "the prohibited
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest" (People v. Honig (1996)
48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333), and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they
might profit from it in any way. (Id.) When the Section 1090 prohibition is applicable, the
prohibition acts as an absolute bar to a board entering into the prohibited contract. (69
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102 (1986).) Nothing in Section 1090 requires you to step down should you
marry the General Manager.

However, once married, you have a financial interest in your spouse's salary and her
employment contract with the District. Accordingly, Section 1090 prohibits you and the Board from
further contracting with your spouse including any changes in her terms of employment under the
existing contract or the renewal of the existing contract unless an exception to Section 1090
applies.³

Spousal Noninterest Exception.

Under Section 1091.5, an official has a noninterest in his or her spouse's employment or
holding office if it has existed for at least one year prior to his or her election or appointment to
office. (Section 1091.5(a)(6).) This exception allows one spouse to retain his or her employment
even though the other spouse was a member of a board that participated in the employment contract
so long as the terms of the employment did not change. (Thorpe, supra, 83 Cal.App.4th at 655.)

___________________________

³ An official has an interest in the community and separate property income of his or her spouse. (Thorpe v.
Long Beach Community College Dist. (2009) 83 Cal.App.4th 655; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 69 (2006).) Thus, a member of
a board or commission is always financially interested in his or her spouse's source of income for purposes of Section
1090. This is true even if the husband and wife have an agreement that their own earnings are to be treated as separate
property, since each spouse is liable for the necessities of life for the other. (Reese v. Alcoholic Bev. Etc. Appeals Bd.
(1976) 64 Cal.App.3d 675, 683).)

File No. A-17-238
Page No. 3

However, this exception does not apply to the facts presented because the General Manager has not held her position for a year prior to your election.4

Rule of Necessity.

The rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed where a conflict of interest exists. The Attorney General's Office has opined that the rule of necessity may allow certain contracts to be made, even when the contract cannot qualify for the spousal noninterest exception. (See 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102 (1986) [concluding a school district may contract on an annual basis with a tenured teacher who was the spouse of a board member, until the board member could qualify for this exception]; 65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305 (1982) [finding a superintendent who was interested in his or her spouse's school employment could utilize the rule of necessity].) In this case, the rule of necessity would not generally permit the Board to change the General Manager's terms of employment. However, if the Board must renew the contract upon its expiration or act to terminate the General Manager's contract, the Board should seek additional advice regarding the application of the rule of necessity.

The Act;

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. Interests from which a disqualifying conflict of interest may arise are enumerated in Section 87103. Based upon the facts provided, the only interest implicated is your interest in your personal finances.5

An official always has an interest in his or her "personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. A decision has a personal financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official (or the official's immediate family) increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103.) Generally, this prohibits an official from taking part in a decision to appoint, hire, fire, promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action against a spouse, or to set a spouse's salary different from salaries paid to employees in the same classification or position. (Regulation 18704(d)(3).) An official may take part in a decision affecting the salary or benefits of his or her spouse only if the decision applies equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or representative group. (See Regulation 18702.5(b)(1).)

4 Note that upon reelection, you would qualify for the exception but must continue to recuse yourself from the decisions implicated by the Act's conflict of interest provisions. (See 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102 (1986).)

5 Under Section 87103(c), an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny source of income ... aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made." Income includes an official's community property interest in his or her spouse's income. However, "salary and reimbursement for expenses" received from a local government agency are not "income" for purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).)

File No. A-17-238
Page No. 4

To the extent that a Board decision implicates your spouse and your spouse's salary, you must be aware that you are disqualified from any decision with a foreseeable and material effect on your personal finances. Accordingly, you are potentially disqualified from taking part in decisions regarding your spouse's duties, performance, pay, and benefits. You should seek additional advice regarding any decision before the Board implicating your spouse or her salary.

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl
